questions.text: Who is the inventor in the story?
questions.explanation: Mr. Willy Wonka is the eccentric inventor of amazing things like Wonka-Vite and Vita-Wonk.

questions.text: What did Wonka-Vite do to people?
questions.explanation: Wonka-Vite was an invention designed to make people young, but it was too strong.

questions.text: What happened to the person who took too much Wonka-Vite?
questions.explanation: The invention was so powerful that one person's age dropped below zero, meaning they 'disappeared' and had to wait 87 years to be born.

questions.text: What was the purpose of the new invention, 'Vita-Wonk'?
questions.explanation: To reverse the effects of Wonka-Vite, Mr. Wonka needed a recipe to make people 'grow old' again.

questions.text: Mr. Wonka traveled across the world in his:
questions.explanation: He used the Great Glass Elevator to visit various parts of the world to collect ingredients.

questions.text: Which tree is mentioned as living the longest?
questions.explanation: Mr. Wonka identifies the Bristlecone Pine, which lives for over 4,000 years, as the oldest living thing.

questions.text: Where can the Bristlecone Pine tree be found today?
questions.explanation: Mr. Wonka tells Charlie that these ancient trees grow on the slopes of Wheeler Peak.

questions.text: Which of these was NOT an ingredient collected by Mr. Wonka?
options: A pint of sap from a 4,000-year-old pine
A cup of fresh milk from a cow
questions.explanation: Mr. Wonka only collected parts of ancient or long-lived creatures; fresh milk was not one of them.

questions.text: What was the name of the cat mentioned in the ingredients?
questions.explanation: The ingredient was 'the whiskers of a 36-year-old cat called Crumpets'.

questions.text: What did Mr. Wonka collect from the Russian Farmer, Petrova?
questions.explanation: He collected an egg-cupful of sugar from the toenail clippings of an 168-year-old Russian farmer.

questions.text: How many drops of the final oily black liquid did the volunteer swallow?
questions.explanation: The brave Oompa-Loompa swallowed four drops of the finished product.

questions.text: Who was the brave volunteer for the test?
questions.explanation: A young, twenty-year-old Oompa-Loompa volunteered to test the new invention.

questions.text: What happened to the volunteer immediately after swallowing the liquid?
options: He began wrinkling and shrivelling up
questions.explanation: The Vita-Wonk worked instantly, making the young man look very old.

questions.text: At what age did the volunteer end up after taking Vita-Wonk?
questions.explanation: The text states that he suddenly became an old fellow of seventy-five.

questions.text: The author of 'Charlie and the Chocolate Factory', from which this is adapted, is:
questions.explanation: Roald Dahl is the famous author who created Willy Wonka.

questions.text: What was the color of the liquid created by Mr. Wonka?
questions.explanation: The final mixture was an 'oily black liquid'.

questions.text: Mr. Wonka said he had to 'mix and boil and bubbled and mixed' the ingredients in his:
questions.explanation: All his great inventions take place in the Inventing Room.

questions.text: What was special about the Knuckle-bones collected in Tibet?
questions.explanation: Mr. Wonka mentions collecting knuckle-bones from a 700-year-old Cattaloo living in Tibet.

questions.text: The story uses many funny, made-up names for animals. Which of these is one?
questions.explanation: Whangdoodles, Hornswogglers, and Snozzwangers are classic Roald Dahl creations.

questions.text: Charlie's role in this excerpt is to:
options: Listen to Mr. Wonka and ask questions
questions.explanation: Charlie acts as the audience, listening to Mr. Wonka's fantastic descriptions.

questions.text: What did Mr. Wonka do when he 'struck upon' the idea of Vita-Wonk?
questions.explanation: He was so excited by the new idea that he literally jumped for joy.

questions.text: What does 'Vita' in Vita-Wonk likely refer to?
questions.explanation: In Latin, 'Vita' means life, often used in things relating to health or age.

questions.text: Why was the Oompa-Loompa's hair falling out after taking the drops?
questions.explanation: Rapid aging involves losing hair and teeth, which happened instantly to the volunteer.

questions.text: Mr. Wonka says he went 'all over the world'. This shows he is:
questions.explanation: He was willing to travel anywhere to find the exact ancient ingredients he needed.

questions.text: How did Charlie react to the list of items Mr. Wonka collected?
questions.explanation: Charlie is consistently wide-eyed and amazed by Mr. Wonka's stories.

questions.text: Which of these is a 'living thing' Mr. Wonka searched for?
questions.explanation: He mentioned collecting things like the stinger of a venomous Squerkle (an insect).

questions.text: What is the tone of the story?
questions.explanation: The story uses exaggerated lists and funny names to create a whimsical atmosphere.

questions.text: What was the age of the tortoise mentioned?
questions.explanation: He collected an egg from a 200-year-old giant tortoise from the Tonga Islands.

questions.text: What is 'Wonka-Vite' compared to?
questions.explanation: It's treated as a powerful medicine that changes biological age.

questions.text: In the end, Mr. Wonka was ________ with his invention.
questions.explanation: He proudly announced to Charlie that 'Vita-Wonk was invented!'

questions.text: Which part of the world did Mr. Wonka NOT mention visiting?
questions.explanation: The text mentions the others, but not Antarctica.

questions.text: What is an 'Oompa-Loompa'?
options: A small person who works for Mr. Wonka
questions.explanation: They are the small, loyal workers in Willy Wonka's factory.

questions.text: What does 'shrivelled up' mean?
questions.explanation: It describes the process of aging or drying out.

questions.text: The story suggests that scientific invention requires:
options: Careful tracking of results and bold experimentation
questions.explanation: Mr. Wonka's tracking of ingredients and the use of a volunteer shows a (whimsical) scientific process.

questions.text: What did Mr. Wonka use to measure the 'drops' of liquid?
questions.explanation: He measured out exactly four drops.

questions.text: How old was the Bristlecone Pine again?
questions.explanation: It is stated clearly as being over 4,000 years old.

questions.text: What happened to the teeth of the Oompa-Loompa?
questions.explanation: Losing teeth is a sign of extreme old age in the story's transformation.

questions.text: Who did Mr. Wonka want to make older?
questions.explanation: He needed to bring back those who had disappeared due to Wonka-Vite.

questions.text: The 'Inventing Room' was full of:
questions.explanation: It was a place of frantic activity, boiling liquids, and machines.

questions.text: What is the moral or theme of this story?
options: Be careful with what you wish for/invent
questions.explanation: The unintended consequences of Wonka-Vite show that interventions in nature can have messy results.
